item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (This is actually the series that Tau-5 originally comes from. They were our team's third-place entry during the 2016 MTF contest.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"… Get Tau-5."

Yes…

YES! YES! YES!
Whoa! This is a huge milestone in terms of tales. Since Avatara was posted, Tau-5 have managed to make some serious waves in the community. Here's to everyone's favorite cyborg murder children.

item.title: Re: Confused
item.content:encoded:
The bolded text in between the lines of the passage- possibly the god the fleshblob was trying to summon answer your questions. The brain matter and gunk was presumably already there- perhaps it was a byproduct of the chanting fleshblob's transformation into a flesh blob.

'sᴀᴛᴇ ɪᴛs ᴛʜɪʀsᴛ'

ɢɪᴠᴇ ɪᴛ ʏᴏᴜʀ ғʟᴇsʜ ᴛʜᴀᴛ ʜᴇ ᴍᴀʏ ᴍᴀᴋᴇ ʏᴏᴜ ɢᴏᴅs

The later sequels to this series clear things up. This particular mtf- Samsara- deal with magical, thaumaturgic, and psionic anomalies, hence they are taking out this sarkic-ish summoner in a farmhouse. The smearing of flesh over his glove before firing is a ritual, a perquisite to harm the entity. Nanku's head was merely an expansion upon that. Wrapping the grenade in a sacrifice of flesh to destroy the entity. She was already affected by the anomalies, sprouting tumors and such so her current body would likely have been terminated anyways. This is just an efficient use of resources.

item.title: Confused
item.content:encoded:
Having trouble following

Confirmed," Irantu noted. He motioned for the group to take a few steps back and fired a few tentative rounds into the mass, which all ricocheted off and into the walls.

Irantu looked at the mass, scooped up some brain and marrow lying around, and coated his glove in it. This time he was able to make a dent in the mass.

Shotgun shot bounced off and didn't hurt it? Then why was there brain matter and bone marrow laying around? What does the last sentence about making a dent this time even mean?

Also, why in the world did they decapitate Nanku just to deliver a grenade internally?
Am excite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, it sounded a lot like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this SCP-253?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An intriguing, interesting introduction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: they are a mtf made from this and an accident caused them to not have very human minds, I think the guy at the end is supposed to have worked for Prometheus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so the mtf IS the clones
I didn't get that, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it! Very poetic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this tale actually introduces and sets up this MTF very well. Does so while being interesting (if a bit icky) as well. I'm looking forward to seeing more from this lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm already in love with these guys; totally thought they were a sarkic task force, due to their odd reaction to the 'child' flesh (and the whole eating their own delicious tumors thing). Can't wait for part 2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm puzzled by their being able to just keep it. Wasn't there any sort of debriefing after the event that would have revealed it's existence if they weren't actively hiding it?

Given their nature, I can get that they wouldn't realize how far out of procedure they are by keeping the book. And the Foundation can't tell them "no" if they don't know the book exists. But they don't seem like they were actively hiding it, and it seems that after an event like this there would be an extensive debriefing (they would want to do everything they could to figure out what caused the anomaly, even though it was neutralized) which should have revealed it by default.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point of clarification: is the book (someone chanting words from the book) was what was causing the neoplasanomoly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes me think of a video game mindset - no need to fear death or wounds since they don't feel pain and are at worst set back by it. Also the distant, uninvolved approach to the situation.

I am mostly wondering about how well they fit the Foundation - Is the way they function understood enough that they trust to use them? It sounds like there would at least be serious debate on whether they shouldn't just be classified as euclid and kept apart - the way they handle the artifact and plan to go on a great adventure would suggest that letting them go free might have been a bad idea. I hope later parts of their story give good reasons for the decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can remedy some of those issues:

The italics section is an origin story of sorts, detailing the original creation of the MTF. It shows four nameless test subjects uploading their minds into a computer to be re-downloaded into grown bodies, experiencing their post-human lives being experimented on, getting their minds erased by the destruction of the facility, and their rediscovery by the Foundation.

The machinery nullifies the "meatgrinder" effect occurring in the farmhouse, allowing the MTF to enter and conduct operations without being turned into pulpy masses of cancer. However, its volatility (read: tendency to explode violently) means that the nonfeeling, respawning MTF is the only group allowed to actually use it.

The book… well that's foreshadowing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh.
I don't like it.
Too much body horror for my taste, the beginning is a good hook but doesn't deliver much, I don't understand the link between the italics and the rest, I don't understand what the machinery they carry in the rucksacks does, I don't understand the book… Really not something for me, sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erm, well, huh…

So this story didn't do much for me. I found the opening scene to be a little cliche and the dialogue to be incredibly clunky. Granted, the main trio don't really know how to be humans, so some clunkiness to their dialogue is to be expected, but everyone in this tale had dialogue that felt kind of robotic and lifeless.

And that is a shame, as I dig the premise of a MTF that the Foundation can literally keep throwing at something because it cannot die.

No vote for now. Fingers crossed the dialogue improves in later installments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the hook at the beginning, and the ended left me wanting more.

I'm guessing these four beings are trying to fuse into something resembling a human? I'm not sure, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what's going on, but I like it. Thought the anomaly was the heart-gathering shadows for a while there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Enthusiastic upvote, eagerly awaiting more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A cautious upvote for now. I'm interested enough to wait for the next part. I do wonder how much of the body horror was necessary. Seemed a tad excessive. But I do like the idea of mutants doing dirty work. Reminds me of the Nurgle mythos but in a good way. Keep it up.

Edit: After a second read, I think the body horror is well justified and not just there for shock value. Still looking forward to more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a very strong hook - a team that doesn't need to fear death or injury, but isn't quite human, is interesting psychological territory - and the writing is clearer than the draft I read (or perhaps I just read it more carefully the second time).

I think I'm stuck at a no-vote for now. Partly because some of the body horror isn't quite clicking with me at the moment (although my tastes may change on that), partly because some of the build-up here feels very withholding (eg the stuff about the book) and partly because while I know the interchangeability of the characters is part of the point, that doesn't make them more interesting. Some hints of individual personalities would have helped, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Winced a little at the "We can't blow it up without finding out what's inside; we both remember Kodiak."; it felt a bit like a blatant "as you know". Chuckled a little at the Survivor Type reference. Overall it was neat, but I never felt really drawn into this. Though maybe it'd look better if I read this when I was more awake. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see you have changed it a bit since I read it on 19. It's still just as good.

Upvoted accordingly

item.title: Introducing Mobile Task Force Tau-5 "Samsara"
item.content:encoded:
Part 1 of a series.

Thanks to my teammates GreenWolf, sirpudding, and TyGently for helping me brainstorm this tale and providing feedback and support. Thanks to MrWrong, WrongJohnSilver, Faminepulse, Lazar Lyusternik, and everyone else in chat for their feedback.

THE SEQUELS:

1. Avatara
2. GRANT REQUEST FOR INVESTIGATING THE APPLICATION OF CERTAIN RESEARCH ASSETS IN OVERCOMING INHERENT LIMITATIONS OF THE HUMAN BODY
3. SCP-2970 (Holy Misplacement)
4. Operation AZURE PEREGRINE
5. The Lord of Endowments
6. Samsara

See more of my work on my author page!
